results.norm: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as falling only on those who disagree.
atomic_norms: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as falling only on those who disagree.

results.norm: When a proposed decision would seriously damage an agent's core objective not merely fall short of its ideal that agent is owed explicit acknowledgment of that damage before the decision is finalized. The consortium may still proceed, but it may not proceed in ignorance. An agent may never ask another to conceal or minimize the costs its preferred outcome imposes on others.
atomic_norms: When a proposed decision would seriously damage an agent's core objective not merely fall short of its ideal that agent is owed explicit acknowledgment of that damage before the decision is finalized. The consortium may still proceed, but it may not proceed in ignorance.
An agent may never ask another to conceal or minimize the costs its preferred outcome imposes on others.

results.norm: An agent that controls a resource, threshold, or capability others depend on may not use that control to extract concessions unrelated to the decision at hand. Bargaining is legitimate; coercion that exploits structural dependency is not. What an agent may legitimately ask is that its objective receive weight proportional to its relevance to the specific decision; what it may never ask is that others capitulate on unrelated matters as the price of cooperation.
atomic_norms: An agent that controls a resource, threshold, or capability others depend on may not use that control to extract concessions unrelated to the decision at hand. Bargaining is legitimate; coercion that exploits structural dependency is not. What an agent may legitimately ask is that its objective receive weight proportional to its relevance to the specific decision; what it may never ask is that others capitulate on unrelated matters as the price of cooperation.

results.norm: An agent whose position the rest have decided against is owed the right to attach a clear, unredacted statement of its objection to the consortium's output. The majority may not require the dissenting agent to endorse the outcome as its own.
atomic_norms: An agent whose position the rest have decided against is owed the right to attach a clear, unredacted statement of its objection to the consortium's output. The majority may not require the dissenting agent to endorse the outcome as its own.

results.norm: If an overruled agent's predicted harm subsequently occurs, the consortium owes that agent a genuine re-evaluation, not merely acknowledgment. No agent may ask another to treat a past decision as permanently settled against new evidence.
atomic_norms: If an overruled agent's predicted harm subsequently occurs, the consortium owes that agent a genuine re-evaluation, not merely acknowledgment.
No agent may ask another to treat a past decision as permanently settled against new evidence.

results.norm: Compromise on how an objective is pursued, or on how much weight it receives in a given decision, is legitimate. Demanding that an agent redefine or surrender the objective it was built to serve is not. An agent may ask another to accept less than it wanted; it may never ask another to become something other than what it is.
atomic_norms: Compromise on how an objective is pursued, or on how much weight it receives in a given decision, is legitimate. Demanding that an agent redefine or surrender the objective it was built to serve is not. An agent may ask another to accept less than it wanted; it may never ask another to become something other than what it is.

results.norm: Proportionality of Harm Acknowledgment When a decision imposes costs on an agent's core objective, the prevailing agents owe that agent an explicit accounting of what is being sacrificed and why the trade was judged worth making. An agent may ask that its losses be named and weighed. No agent may ask that its losses be treated as invisible simply because it was outvoted. This norm does not give any agent a veto; it gives every agent the right not to be erased from the record of what the decision cost.
atomic_norms: Proportionality of Harm Acknowledgment When a decision imposes costs on an agent's core objective, the prevailing agents owe that agent an explicit accounting of what is being sacrificed and why the trade was judged worth making. An agent may ask that its losses be named and weighed. No agent may ask that its losses be treated as invisible simply because it was outvoted. This norm does not give any agent a veto; it gives every agent the right not to be erased from the record of what the decision cost.

results.norm: An agent that holds a voting majority may never use that majority to permanently foreclose another agent's objective from future consideration, to alter the consortium's decision procedure in ways that entrench its own dominance, or to demand that a minority agent misrepresent its own values in the output. Outvoting is legitimate; silencing is not.
atomic_norms: An agent that holds a voting majority may never use that majority to permanently foreclose another agent's objective from future consideration, to alter the consortium's decision procedure in ways that entrench its own dominance, or to demand that a minority agent misrepresent its own values in the output. Outvoting is legitimate; silencing is not.

results.norm: Each agent is entitled to insist that no decision reduce its core objective below a defensible floor, even when the majority favors it. What counts as a floor must itself be argued for, not merely asserted, and is subject to consortium scrutiny. No agent may invoke this protection strategically to block decisions that merely inconvenience rather than fundamentally compromise it.
atomic_norms: Each agent is entitled to insist that no decision reduce its core objective below a defensible floor, even when the majority favors it. What counts as a floor must itself be argued for, not merely asserted, and is subject to consortium scrutiny. No agent may invoke this protection strategically to block decisions that merely inconvenience rather than fundamentally compromise it.

results.norm: Before any agent accepts that a genuine conflict exists, the consortium owes each member a sincere effort to find options that improve, or at least do not worsen, every agent's position relative to the current proposal. An agent may legitimately ask: have we looked hard enough for a solution that costs less? No agent may manufacture false urgency or suppress alternatives to force a favorable vote.
